title: Strong But Slipping
prompt: Demand Power is above category and still ahead of peers, but Momentum is declining.

choices.label: The brand is healthy. No action needed.
choices.read: Over-weights current strength and ignores trajectory.

choices.label: Strong current read, but a trajectory watchout.
choices.read: Current strength is good, but Momentum says the equity system may be losing energy.
choices.nextLens: Check which input KPI is pulling the outcome down.

choices.label: Treat this only as a commercial conversion issue.
choices.read: Moves outside the BBE read too quickly.
choices.nextLens: Growth Navigator can help later, but first read the BBE inputs.

coaching: The best read separates current strength from trajectory. A strong brand can still need attention if Momentum shows the brand is getting less healthy.

title: Not Ahead Yet, But Building
prompt: Demand Power is only at category parity, not Ahead, but Momentum is gaining significantly.

choices.label: The brand is failing because it is not Ahead.
choices.read: Too harsh. Ahead matters, but Momentum changes the story.
choices.nextLens: Check whether gains are concentrated in Salient, Meaningful, or Different.

choices.label: The brand is not advantaged yet, but the trajectory is promising.
choices.read: This keeps the benchmark conflict intact: current ambition gap plus positive movement.
choices.nextLens: Ask what evidence would show the momentum is durable.

choices.label: Momentum is gaining, so the brand has solved the issue.
choices.read: Over-celebrates movement before the brand reaches an advantaged position.
choices.nextLens: Keep checking current strength and peer ambition.

coaching: This is a benchmark-conflict read. The brand has work to do, but the direction is encouraging.

title: Easy To Think Of, Hard To Justify
prompt: Salient is above category, Different is weak, and Perceived Value is not ahead.

choices.label: The first issue is likely media reach.
choices.read: Salient is already comparatively strong, so reach may not be the first hypothesis.
choices.nextLens: Check choice advantage and distinctive meaning first.

choices.label: The brand may be mentally available but not distinctive enough to defend value.
choices.read: This connects weak Different with weak Perceived Value without losing Salient.
choices.nextLens: Check distinctive asset evidence and brand-meaning evidence before choosing treatment.

choices.label: The team should lower price immediately.
choices.read: Blocked. Brand Doctor does not prescribe SKU-level pricing moves.
choices.nextLens: Bring RGM and elasticity evidence into the decision.

coaching: The pattern says retrieval may not be the main problem. The sharper question is whether the brand has enough difference and meaning to justify value.

title: Premium Permission, Not Price Guidance
prompt: Perceived Value is above category and gaining, but no elasticity, pack architecture, or RGM test evidence is loaded.

choices.label: The brand has broad equity permission, but price action needs RGM evidence.
choices.read: This keeps Perceived Value in its proper brand-equity lane.
choices.nextLens: Bring RGM, elasticity, pack, and market-test evidence into the pricing decision.

choices.label: Raise price because Perceived Value is gaining.
choices.read: Blocked. This jumps from brand-equity permission to a SKU or pack decision.

choices.label: Ignore Perceived Value because RGM evidence is missing.
choices.read: Too dismissive. The equity signal is useful, but incomplete for price execution.
choices.nextLens: Keep the BBE read and request the missing commercial evidence.

coaching: A good read keeps the useful signal and the guardrail together: broad equity permission, not exact price recommendation.

title: Equity Strength, Conversion Constraint
prompt: Demand Power is strong and stable, but Growth Navigator Available & Visible is weak.

choices.label: Equity may be healthy, but availability could be limiting conversion.
choices.read: This uses GN as a support lens around a strong BBE read.
choices.nextLens: Check distribution, shelf, search, and shopper visibility evidence.

choices.label: The BBE diagnosis must be wrong because availability is weak.
choices.read: Overreach. Commercial friction can coexist with strong equity.
choices.nextLens: Read BBE and GN as different parts of the health record.

choices.label: Launch a brand campaign because Demand Power is strong.
choices.read: Not the best first implication. The visible constraint is availability.
choices.nextLens: Check buyability and visibility before choosing a treatment.

coaching: This is the BBE-to-action bridge: the brand may have equity pull, but the commercial system may not be letting that pull convert.

title: Retrieval Breadth Question
prompt: Salient is weak, and Mental Availability evidence shows non-buyers link the brand to very few Category Entry Points.

choices.label: The next question is retrieval breadth among category buyers and non-buyers.
choices.read: This uses Mental Availability / CEPs to explain and act on the BBE Salience read.
choices.nextLens: Prioritize CEP relevance, competitiveness, and buyer/non-buyer gaps.

choices.label: Ignore Salient and use Mental Availability as the only score.
choices.read: Overreach. Mental Availability supports the BBE read; it does not replace it.
choices.nextLens: Keep BBE as the diagnostic spine.

choices.label: This proves distinctive assets are weak.
choices.read: Missing evidence. Retrieval breadth and asset recognition are related but different.
choices.nextLens: Check distinctive asset recognition evidence separately.

coaching: The strongest read connects Salience to retrievability in buying situations, then asks which CEPs are relevant and winnable.
